name: ASDL
summary: Wang, Appel, Korn, Serra (1997): a language for describing tree-shaped data with sums, products, and optional fields. From one ASDL file, generators emit typed Python, C, C++, ML, or Java accessors. Predates ungrammar by two decades.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: ASDL (Wang, Appel, Korn, Serra; 1997) is a language for describing tree-shaped data with sums, products, and optional fields. From one ASDL file, generators emit typed Python, C, C++, ML, or Java accessors. CPython's `Python.asdl` defines the canonical Python AST shape; Oil/OSH derived its lossless tree from ASDL; SML/NJ used it internally; CompCert uses an ASDL-like spec for its IR languages. ASDL predates ungrammar by two decades and occupies the same design slot.

sources.label: ASDL — Abstract Syntax Description Language (Wang, Appel, Korn, Serra)

sections.title: Schema as the source of truth
sections.content:
The architectural pattern: a single ASDL file is authoritative. Visitors, type checkers, serializers, and language bindings are all generated from it rather than reinvented per consumer. CPython's `Python.asdl` is the canonical example — every change to Python's AST shape changes one file, and every consumer (the C parser, the AST printer, the disassembler, third-party tools) gets regenerated.

This pattern is what ungrammar adopts at the rust-analyzer scale, and what every modern language-tooling project that wants schema-driven CST/AST infrastructure rediscovers.
